chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in infants under six months with major comorbidities, including TB, HIV, neurodisability, congenital heart disease, CKD, and CLD, emphasizing tailored feeding, monitoring, and treatment adjustments.
learning_objectives: Understand how comorbidities modify the standard SAM management pathway in infants under six months.
Learn specific adjustments for feeding, fluid thresholds, and antibiotic choices based on dominant comorbidities.
Recognize the importance of early screening and referral for conditions like TB, HIV, and congenital heart disease.
Develop skills in caregiver education for safe feeding practices in neurodisability cases.
key_facts: TB management: Screen non-responders early; pyridoxine prophylaxis is essential with isoniazid regimens.
HIV management: Start ART after stabilizing sepsis; add high-dose cotrimoxazole for suspected PCP pneumonia.
Neurodisability: Use slower feeds, head elevation, and NG support; caregiver training is mandatory for discharge.
Congenital heart disease: Escalate feeds slowly, restrict fluids, and monitor respiratory rate during feed advancement.
CKD/CLD: Avoid nephrotoxic antibiotics; reduce phosphate replacement in suspected refeeding syndrome.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Maintain a low threshold for screening in non-responders; do not rely on a negative TST. Coordinate diagnostics and follow-up with NTEP. (Reference 3)
document_key_topics: Severe acute malnutrition in infants under six months
Management modifications for TB and HIV comorbidities
Feeding and monitoring adjustments for neurodisability and congenital heart disease
Guidelines for CKD and CLD in SAM management
